title: Fruit rollups
subtitle: Mostly waiting, mostly worth it.
excerpt: Fruit rollups. A home-cook's recipe from Rebecca's collection, kept in the family rotation.

content.text: Fruit rollups are strips of dried fruit puree, peeled from parchment and rolled up. Blend the fruit smooth, spread it thin, and dry it low and slow in the oven until the centre is no longer tacky. The result is chewy, intensely fruity, and portable.

The key is patience with the drying time. Thickness and water content in the fruit both affect how long it takes. Check from 4 hours and keep going until the whole sheet feels set and dry to the touch.
Blend all ingredients in a food processor or blender until smooth. Taste and adjust sweetness.
Pour into a baking tray lined with parchment paper and spread out to about 3 to 6 mm thick.

Dry in the oven at 60 to 75°C (or as low as your oven goes) for 4 to 6 hours, until the centre is no longer tacky. The whole sheet must be fully set before you try to peel it. Thicker layers take longer; keep checking until the centre feels dry.
Leave to cool completely, then use clean scissors to trim the edges and cut into strips about 2.5 cm wide. Roll each strip into a rollup.
Tropical: use pineapple and mango for a brighter, more fragrant result.
Forest fruit: blend cherries, blueberries, and raspberries for a deep colour and sharp flavour.
Dehydrator: if you have a food dehydrator, follow its instructions instead of the oven step.
Quicker drying: boil the blended mixture for 5 to 10 minutes to drive off excess liquid before spreading. This cuts the drying time noticeably.

Store in an airtight container in the fridge for up to 2 weeks. A piece of kitchen paper in the container helps absorb moisture. They also keep well at room temperature for the day if you take them out. To freeze: store in an airtight container for up to 3 months. Thaw in the fridge overnight before eating.
Frozen fruit: let it thaw first, then strain out as much liquid as possible before blending. Canned fruit: drain well and dry it further with kitchen paper before blending.

items.fix: Keep drying until the centre is completely dry to the touch. Thickness matters: thicker layers take longer. If the edges are done but the centre is not, tent loosely with foil over the edges.
items.cause: Not dried long enough, or layer too thick
items.symptom: Rollup is sticky and won't peel cleanly

items.fix: Strain out as much liquid as possible before blending. You can also boil the mixture briefly to reduce it before spreading.
items.symptom: Takes much longer than 6 hours to dry

items.fix: Spread more evenly or pour less batter per tray. The edges dry faster than the centre.
items.cause: Layer was too thin at the edges
items.symptom: Edges crisp and crack while centre is still tacky

content.text: A home cook's recipe. It sits among the things families make without a fixed formula, adjusting each time based on whatever fruit needs using up.
